items.title: Classic duodenal ulcer triad
items.contentHtml:
Classic duodenal ulcer triad

  • Epigastric pain that improves with food, wakes the patient up 2-3 hours after sleeping and is relieved with antacids.
  • The pain is burning or burning, located in the epigastrium, and typically appears 90-180 minutes after meals.
  • The triad has sensitivity ~70% but low specificity; its absence does not rule out an ulcer.
  • Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma) should be suspected if there are multiple, refractory ulcers or in atypical locations.

items.foco: Epigastric pain that improves with food
items.title: Epigastric pain that improves with food
items.contentHtml:
Epigastric pain that improves with food

  • It is the most distinctive clinical finding of duodenal ulcer (vs. gastric ulcer where the pain usually worsens with food).
  • Mechanism: food buffers the acid and dilutes gastric secretion, temporarily relieving the pain.
  • The pain reappears 2-3 hours later when the acid returns and the stomach is empty.
  • In gastric ulcer, pain may appear immediately after eating and does not improve with food; This guides mandatory biopsy due to risk of malignancy.

items.foco: H. pylori: urease and virulence factors
items.title: H. pylori: urease and virulence factors
items.contentHtml:
H. pylori: urease and virulence factors

  • Urease: key enzyme that hydrolyzes urea into ammonia, generating an alkaline microenvironment that allows survival at gastric pH.
  • Main virulence factors: CagA (associated with increased risk of gastric cancer and peptic ulcer) and VacA (vacuolization of epithelial cells).
  • Adhesins (BabA, SabA) facilitate colonization of the mucosa.
  • The rapid urease test in gastric biopsy detects urease activity in <1 hour; sensitivity >90% if there is active colonization.

items.title: Marked urea breath test
items.contentHtml:
Labeled urea breath test

  • Uses urea labeled with 13C or 14C; ifH. pylori is present, urease hydrolyzes urea and marked CO2 is detected in the breath.
  • Sensitivity and specificity >95%; It is the non-invasive test of choice for diagnosis and to confirm post-treatment eradication (wait ≥4 weeks after finishing antibiotics).
  • It requires fasting for 4-6 hours and suspending PPI (at least 2 weeks) and antibiotics (4 weeks) prior to the test.
  • It should not be used in patients with gastrectomy (false negatives due to alteration anatomical).

items.foco: Fecal antigen for diagnosis of H. pylori
items.title: Fecal antigen for diagnosis of H. pylori
items.contentHtml:
Fecal antigen for diagnosis of H. pylori

  • Detects H. pylori antigens in feces by ELISA or immunochromatography.
  • Sensitivity and specificity ~90-95%, comparable to the breath test.
  • Useful for initial diagnosis and control of eradication (like the breath test, wait ≥4 weeks post-treatment).
  • Advantage: does not require fasting or specialized equipment; disadvantage: lower accuracy in active digestive bleeding (false negatives).
  • Should not be used if the patient has taken PPI in the last 2 weeks or antibiotics in the last 4 weeks.

items.title: Triple therapy: PPI, amoxicillin, clarithromycin
items.contentHtml:
Triple therapy: PPI, amoxicillin, clarithromycin

  • First-line regimen for eradication of H. pylori in areas with resistance to clarithromycin <15%.
  • Standard dose: PPI (standard dose every 12 hours) + amoxicillin 1 g every 12 hours + clarithromycin 500 mg every 12 hours for 14 days.
  • If the patient is allergic to penicillin, replace amoxicillin with metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours (quadruple therapy with bismuth or sequential therapy).
  • Eradication rate with classic triple therapy: ~80-90% if resistance <15%; if resistance >15%, use quadruple therapy with bismuth (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline).

items.title: Gastric ulcer: risk of malignancy
items.contentHtml:
Gastric ulcer: risk of malignancy

  • Every gastric ulcer should be biopsied during esophagogastroduodenoscopy (minimum 6-8 edge and fundal biopsies) to rule out gastric adenocarcinoma or MALT lymphoma.
  • The risk of malignancy in gastric ulcer is ~2-5%; in duodenal ulcer it is <1% (does not require routine biopsy).
  • Alarm characteristics: weight loss, iron deficiency anemia, dysphagia, persistent vomiting, epigastric mass.
  • If the ulcer does not heal after 8-12 weeks of treatment with PPI, repeat endoscopy with biopsies.

items.title: Hemoglobin and peptic ulcer bleeding
items.contentHtml:
Hemoglobin and peptic ulcer bleeding

  • Initial hemoglobin does not reflect the magnitude of the acute loss; hemodilution occurs after 24-72 hours.
  • The BUN/creatinine ratio >30:1 suggests upper digestive bleeding (degradation of blood proteins in the intestine).
  • In active hemorrhage, urgent endoscopy (within 12-24 hours) is the standard; The Rockall scale and Blatchford stratify the risk of rebleeding and mortality.
  • The transfusion should be restrictive (Hb <7 g/dL) in stable patients, and <9 g/dL in those with cardiovascular disease.

items.title: Perforation: pneumoperitoneum on x-ray
items.contentHtml:
Perforation: pneumoperitoneum on radiography

  • Pneumoperitoneum (subdiaphragmatic free air) is the classic radiological finding of peptic ulcer perforation.
  • It is detected on standing chest x-ray or abdomen in left lateral decubitus; sensitivity ~70-80%.
  • If the x-ray is negative but there is high suspicion, request abdominal CT with oral contrast (higher sensitivity).
  • duodenal ulcer perforation is more common on the anterior surface; gastric perforation may present pneumoperitoneum or encapsulated pneumoperitoneum.

items.title: Gastroesophageal reflux and peptic ulcer
items.contentHtml:
Gastroesophageal reflux and peptic ulcer

  • They are different entities; peptic ulcer is located in the stomach or duodenum, while gastroesophageal reflux (GERD) affects the distal esophagus.
  • The GERD pain is retrosternal, with acid regurgitation and worsens when lying down; ulcer pain is epigastric and is related to food.
  • H. pylori infection does not cause GERD; in fact, eradication may worsen reflux in some patients (decreased body gastritis).
  • PPI therapy is common for both, but the duration and need for diagnostic testing (endoscopy) differ.

items.foco: NSAIDs as a cause of gastric ulcer
items.title: NSAIDs as a cause of gastric ulcer
items.contentHtml:
NSAIDs as a cause of gastric ulcer

  • NSAIDs inhibit COX-1 and COX-2, reducing the synthesis of prostaglandins that protect the gastric mucosa.
  • The risk of gastric ulcer is 3-5 times greater in chronic users; the risk of complications (bleeding, perforation) is dose-dependent.
  • Risk factors: age >65 years, history of ulcer, concomitant use of anticoagulants/antiplatelets, high doses and use of multiple NSAIDs.
  • Prophylaxis: PPI or misoprostol in high-risk patients; COXIB reduce the risk of ulcers but do not eliminate it.

items.title: Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy
items.contentHtml:
Esophagogastroduodenoscopy with biopsy

  • It is the gold standard for diagnosis of peptic ulcer and detection of H. pylori.
  • Biopsies of the antrum and gastric body are taken for rapid urease test, histology and culture (if there is antibiotic resistance).
  • In gastric ulcer, biopsy edges and bottom (minimum 6-8) to rule out malignancy; in duodenal ulcer, biopsy is not routine.
  • Endoscopy is indicated in: dysphagia, iron deficiency anemia, weight loss, persistent vomiting, digestive bleeding or treatment failure.
